concerns.text: FR-002 mandates replacing the 0.6B neural encoder with a 'linear projection layer' mapping AST feature vectors to the original embedding dimension. This is scientifically unsound: AST features (e.g., cyclomatic complexity, token counts) are low-dimensional, sparse, and lack the semantic manifold structure of neural embeddings. A linear projection cannot recover the non-linear semantic relationships required for code generation tasks, making the hypothesis that 'syntactic metrics alone preserve performance' untestable via this architecture. The design conflates dimensionality reduction with semantic substitution.

SC-002 proposes measuring exact-match scores against the neural baseline to quantify 'performance retention.' However, if the architecture (FR-002) fundamentally lacks the capacity to encode semantic intent (as AST features do not capture API semantics or logic flow), any performance drop is a tautological consequence of the design, not an empirical finding about the 'sufficiency of syntactic metrics.' The validation target (exact-match on semantic tasks) is definitionally dependent on semantic information that the predictor (AST features) explicitly excludes, rendering the comparison invalid for the stated research question.

SC-005 requires a paired t-test to validate the performance drop. While statistically valid for comparing means, this method assumes the data points are independent and identically distributed (i.i.d.). In code evolution tasks, repositories often share structural similarities and the test set (RepoPeftBench) may have inherent correlations. A simple paired t-test may violate independence assumptions if the 'pairs' (AST vs. Neural adapters) are generated from the same repository context but evaluated on tasks with shared semantic dependencies. A non-parametric test (e.g., Wilcoxon signed-rank) or permutation test would be more robust for this specific data structure.
